titleStmt.title: The Ambassador in China ( (Gauss) ) to the Secretary of State

div.head: The Ambassador in China ( Gauss ) to the Secretary of State
div.p: 721. General Hearn told me some days ago in strict confidence for my ears alone that he had delivered to Madame Chiang, in accordance with usual Army custom, a message from the President to Generalissimo which was rather frank and positive in tone on an important military matter; that he had reason to believe that the message was not promptly delivered to Chiang by Madame; that Madame even sought to have the message “toned down”; and that Chiang’s chief military aide when approached asserted that he was not aware of receipt by Chiang of any message from President on the general subject in question. A few days later Hearn told me the message must finally have reached Chiang in some form or other. Madame had told Hearn that Chiang would not reply directly to President but action in matter would be taken and made known to our military through Chinese military channels.

I do not know whether Hearn has reported this matter to War DepartmentFor the text of President Roosevelt’s message of April 3, 1944, to Generalissimo Chiang Kai-shek and an exposition from the military viewpoint on the delivery of this particular message, see Charles F. Romanus and Riley Sunderland, United States Army in World War II: China–Burma–India Theater: Stilwell’s Command Problems (Washington, Government Printing Office, 1956), pp. 310–311. but I believe it should be known to Secretary of State.
It is known that for some months Chiang has been in an irascible mood and difficult in his relations with his Ministers and others.…

I told Hearn that Embassy does not deliver messages to Chiang through Madame; that when instructed to deliver them personally we do so although at times there is delay in obtaining an appointment; and when not delivered personally they are sent through Foreign Office, either sealed or unsealed as contents of message might dictate. We seldom prepare a Chinese translation, for reasons of security. Chiang has ample staff for the purpose.
